items.fix: Use validation (or cross-validation on train) for all comparisons; evaluate the final model on the test set once.
items.why: Each test-set peek leaks information into model selection — the test score becomes optimistically biased and no longer estimates true generalization.
items.example: Wrong: pick learning rate by best test accuracy over 20 values. Right: pick by validation accuracy, then report test accuracy once for the winner.

items.mistake: Splitting after fitting a scaler or imputer on the full dataset
items.fix: Split first; fit preprocessing on X_train only; transform validation and test with train-fitted artifacts.
items.why: Statistics learned from validation/test rows leak target distribution information into training — held-out metrics overstate performance.
items.example: Wrong: StandardScaler().fit(X_all) then train_test_split. Right: split, then scaler.fit(X_train), scaler.transform(X_val).

items.mistake: Using a random row split when entities repeat across rows
items.fix: Split by group (user_id, patient_id, session_id) so all rows for one entity stay in a single partition.
items.why: Entity-level patterns leak across train and hold-out when the same user appears in both — validation and test scores look better than production on new users.
items.example: Wrong: shuffle 1M click rows randomly. Right: GroupShuffleSplit on user_id, then train/val/test within held-out groups.

items.mistake: Shuffling time-ordered data into random train/val/test
items.fix: Use chronological splits — train on earlier periods, validate and test on later ones.
items.why: Random shuffling puts future observations in training — the model sees the future during fit, and temporal generalization is untested.
items.example: Wrong: train_test_split on daily sales rows. Right: train through 2022, validate 2023 Q1, test 2023 Q2.
